retrieved_docs.content: 6. Indications: Fixed prosthesis: crown, bridges, implant Maxillofacial prosthodontics Registering occlusion Polyether Polyether has become popular because it only requires a stock tray and a single mix. 1. Mode of supply: Available as light, medium, and heavy body consistencies. 2. Composition: The base contains polyether, fillers, and plasticizers. The accelerator contains alkyl aromatic sulfonates, inert oils, and plasticizers. 3. Setting reaction: When base and accelerator are mixed in equal proportions, polyether rubber is formed. 4. Indications Fixed prosthodontics (crown and bridgework, particularly for multiple preparations) Implants Border molding in complete denture fabrication Indirect inlay and onlay impressions It can be used for corrective impressions as it can be added to previously set material. 5. Advantages:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Impression Taking
retrieved_docs.content: When placing the tray in the mouth, retract one side of the lip using a mouth mirror or gloved finger. [9] Insert the tray in the mouth from the side opposite retraction. [9] Center the tray handle and hold the tray in place using light pressure until the material sets; excess pressure during setting results in strain. [9] Relieve the labial flange by manipulating the lips.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 4. Manipulation: The water is added first to the rubber bowl, followed by the powder, as specified by the manufacturer. The mixture is mixed vigorously against the walls of the bowl in a figure 8 motion. The mixing time is 45-60 seconds according to the type of alginate. Then the mixture is loaded into the selected impression tray (tray border should be a maximum of 3 mm short of vestibular depth) and placed into the patient's dental arch. After the alginate attains sufficient elasticity, the impression is removed. There should be a minimum of 3 mm-thick material between the tray and tissues so they do not get torn. The impression should be removed swiftly, simultaneously breaking the seal by inserting fingers in the buccal vestibular region. The impression must be cast within half an hour, as hydrocolloids tend to deform because of imbibition and syneresis. [14] The impression could be kept in 100% humidity and cast within 24 hours instead. But for optimum dimensional stability, an alginate impression should be poured immediately. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: First, the impression tray is selected and prepared with tray adhesive. The impression material is mixed, manually, or by mechanical means. The impression technique may vary according to the case, including multiple mix, monophase, or putty wash techniques. [25] Digital Impressions

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrophilic Accurate and high-dimensional stability enables the delay of casting and allows for multiple pourings. Good elastic recovery Good compatibility with gypsum Good shelf life It can be used as a single-phase material or with a syringe tray technique 6. Disadvantage: The most rigid of all the elastomers makes it hard to remove (newer polyethers are slightly more flexible) May cause allergy due to sulfonate acid esters The impression should not be immersed in disinfectant for more than 10 minutes since it's hydrophilic. [22] [23] Since it absorbs water, it should not be kept with alginate impressions. Polysulfide Polysulfides are the first elastomers to be introduced. Although messy to manipulate, it is useful when a long working time is needed. Polysulfides are not recommended except for complete dentures. 1. Consistencies: light or heavy body 2. Mode of supply: It is available in a 2-paste system: base and accelerator.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Selection of Correct Tray
retrieved_docs.content: Trays for alginate impressions must have retention, such as perforations. [3] The tray chosen for dental arch impression must be the correct shape and size. Correct sizing allows for the optimum 4 to 6 mm cross-sectional thickness of the alginate. [5] Alginic adhesives, available as spray or paint, can also be applied to maximize the material retention in the tray. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dustless alginates: manufactured to prevent inhalation of alginate particles, which may cause respiratory problems. The alginate particles are coated with glycol or glycerine to make them denser. Chromatic alginates: change colors during manipulation to guide mixing time and setting time. In the form of sol or paste (containing water) and a reactor (plaster of Paris) separately. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. Agar (gelling agent) Borax (strengthener) Potassium sulfate (gypsum hardener) Alkyl benzoate (preservatives) Water (reaction medium > 80%) Fillers, flavoring, and coloring agents

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: In the sitting position, the patient should have the back of their knees as close to the edge of the table as possible. The patient should then be instructed to touch their chin to their chest with their hands resting on their thighs and then slouch, pushing the belly button back towards the administering provider. A supporting staff member should be present to safely assist and stabilize the patient, preventing a forward lean, and possible fall from the table. Positional aids such as pillows and stands may also be used as needed if available.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: In the lateral decubitus position, laterality is chosen based comfort of the patient and on having adequate room for support personnel and the proceduralist. Similar to when in the sitting position, the patient should be positioned with their back closest to the administering provider. The patient should be instructed to draw their thighs towards their mid-section while pushing their belly button out towards the administering provider, thus producing spinal flexion. With the help of a supporting staff member, a neutral rotational position should be maintained, and the patient stabilized once properly positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: Halo Brace -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The patient lies supine on a bed/gurney, with the head positioned 8 to 10 inches beyond the edge supported by a head positioner, typically included in a halo application kit.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Immobilization Techniques -- Issues of Concern -- Blocks
retrieved_docs.content: Blocks have been part of the inline mobilization strategy for stabilization of the spine it seems to be effective when strapping the patient on a spinal board of achieving a degree of immobilization with no added benefit of using the hard collar in conjunction [21]

retrieved_docs.content: A base and a catalyst typically constitute elastomers. The light body comes in a syringe, the regular body is available in collapsible tubes, and the putty is dispensed in 2 jars. The mixing can be done manually or by automix systems, eg, disposable intraoral syringes, automatic mixing machines, and dispensers with attached cartridges. retrieved_docs.content: 2. Mode of supply: In collapsible tubes as a base and a catalyst (medium body) In jars as a base and a catalyst (putty) Syringes (ultralow and low)

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Manipulation: Type 1 sheet impression compound is softened in a water bath with warm water and is placed in a stock tray to copy edentulous ridges. The water bath temperature is kept at around 55-60° Celsius, above the impression compound's fusion temperature. [6] The impression compound is a mucocompressive material, meaning that it impresses the mucosa when subjected to compression. These impressions must be corrected with a second impression made in a custom impression tray fabricated on the primary cast of the patient. The type 1 stick impression compound is softened over a flame and placed on the borders of the custom impression tray. [7] The type 2 impression compound is used to create an impression tray for making a wash impression. 6. Properties:

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Imaging -- Patient Positioning
retrieved_docs.content: Patient positioning is relatively straightforward for plain films and ultrasound. The patient should sit on a chair or stool next to the imaging table for radiographs and assume a comfortable position on a gurney or chair with the wrist supported on a pillow or tray for ultrasound evaluation. Patient comfort is paramount for maximum patient cooperation in remaining motionless for plain films and ergonomically accessible to the technologist for US imaging. Nuclear medicine positioning for dedicated wrist imaging is similar to plain film or ultrasound. The patient rests their hands on a gamma camera while sitting in a nearby chair. [49] [50] [51]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: method employed during labor, reflecting the importance of maternal positioning in facilitating a safe birth. Overall, the interconnected network of procedures, methods, and complications underscores the complexity of labor and delivery care. Recognizing how each element relates to the central concept of labor aids clinicians in anticipating potential risks, selecting appropriate interventions, and optimizing outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2394
retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure that is performed on the uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2124
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is classified as an other delivery procedure, placing it within the broader category of delivery interventions used in obstetric care to manage fetal positioning and facilitate safe childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: positioning methods, and a spectrum of complications—including cervical dystocia, uterine atony, prolonged or stimulated labor, and pelvic‑malformation‑related difficult deliveries—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding labor‑related care and its associated indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: or stimulated labor and may result in fetal demise or stillbirth. Abnormal labor, including “مخاض غير طبيعي,” is connected to the SMQ “termination of pregnancy and risk of miscarriage,” which itself is a subcategory of stillbirth, linking placental hemorrhagic events to fetal loss. Delivery techniques such as the sitting position without inversion are recognized as methods that can affect labor progress and maternal comfort. Overall, the interrelated concepts of normal versus abnormal labor, placental previa and other placental pathologies, hemorrhagic and coagulopathic risks, pelvic anatomical abnormalities, and specific delivery positions constitute a comprehensive framework for optimizing patient care during labor, delivery, and the antenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery are classified as a subset of other procedures to assist delivery, indicating that these specific interventions are encompassed within the broader category of techniques used to facilitate and support the childbirth process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 485
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities. A particular delivery method linked to الولادة is وضعية المَجيء المقعدي من دون ذكر الانقلاب (seated delivery position without mention of rotation), illustrating that specific maternal positions are considered within the management of labor. Overall, the knowledge network connects normal delivery pathways with a range of labor complications, especially those arising from pelvic malformations, and underscores the importance of recognizing these relationships for appropriate clinical intervention and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: care. Collectively, these entities form an integrated network of obstetric complications, procedural interventions, positional techniques, multiple‑gestation classifications, neonatal screening considerations, and database linkages that support comprehensive maternal‑fetal risk assessment, research, and optimal patient‑care strategies in obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: temporal occurrence during the fetal period, providing a detailed framework for understanding how diverse maternal and fetal conditions can impact labor and delivery outcomes and guiding clinicians in assessing and managing these complex scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Internal obstetrical version and manual conversion of position are both specific procedures classified as surgical manipulation techniques. Each of these methods is considered a child procedure within the broader category of surgical manipulation, reflecting their role as specialized interventions used to alter fetal position during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: intervention hierarchy. Membrane sweeping and other obstetric techniques employ artificial rupture of membranes to facilitate delivery. The term “medical procedure on cervix” corresponds to العملية في عنق الرحم, denoting a cervical intervention. Collectively, these entities and their hierarchical, equivalence, and anatomical relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the spectrum of obstetric and uterine interventions used to modify fetal position, assist labor, and manage pregnancy termination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Normal delivery procedure is defined as a process that includes the event of الولادة (labor/delivery). The concept of Normal pregnancy & or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery) represents a narrower, more specific relationship to الولادة. During الولادة, several complications may occur, including تعسر المخاض (difficult labor), عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم (cervical dystocia), and ونى الرحم (uterine atony). الولادة can also be characterized by مخاض طويل (prolonged labor) or مخاض محفز (augmented labor). One recommended method for managing الولادة is the وضعية المَجيء المقعدي من دون ذكر الانقلاب (sitting descent position without specifying rotation). Recognizing these interrelated concepts aids clinicians in anticipating labor difficulties and selecting appropriate delivery techniques to improve patient care.
